metadata.title: Professor-Style History Essay Grading Heuristics (World War II)
metadata.intendedUse: Grounding knowledge source for an AI Agent acting as a history professor in Microsoft Copilot Studio.
metadata.scope: Grades a single student history essay against explicit requirements and produces a grading-accuracy self-audit.
metadata.license: Rubric text is original; historical events are public domain.

assignment.topic: Causes, turning points, and lasting consequences of World War II

subject.title: World War II (1939–1945)
subject.scope: European and Pacific theaters, political causes, military strategy, home front, and post-war order
subject.spoilerPolicy: Not applicable. Historical events are factual.

assignment.promptToStudent: Write a formal analytical essay that argues a clear thesis about the causes, key turning points, or lasting consequences of World War II. Support your thesis with primary and secondary historical sources and analysis.

secondaryConstraints: Secondary sources must be peer-reviewed or from reputable historical publishers.
Do not use encyclopedias (e.g., Wikipedia) as primary analytical sources.

wordCount.measurement: Count words in the essay body excluding title page, references, and appendices.

penalties.reason: Too short to meet assignment depth.
Exceeds hard limit; conciseness requirement violated.

notes: At least 2 primary source references (speeches, treaties, documents, diaries).
At least 3 secondary source references (historians, scholarly analyses).

requiredSections.name: Historical Background
mustInclude: key treaties or failures of diplomacy

requiredSections.name: Cause Analysis
mustInclude: at least one primary source reference

requiredSections.name: Key Turning Points
mustInclude: at least 2 major turning points with dates

requiredSections.name: Consequences & Legacy
mustInclude: long-term geopolitical consequences (Cold War, UN, etc.)

requiredSections.name: Source Evidence (Integrated)
mustInclude: each citation introduced and followed by analysis

requiredSections.name: Counterargument / Historiographical Debate
mustInclude: at least one alternative historical interpretation

requiredSections.name: Personal Reflection (Academic Tone)
mustInclude: connection to modern geopolitics or conflict
a specific historical moment that shaped your view

requiredSections.name: Conclusion
mustInclude: final implication or call to continued study

hardFailConditions.description: Substantial plagiarism or copy/paste from existing essays or summaries.
hardFailConditions.notes: If detected, fail the assignment regardless of content quality.

hardFailConditions.description: Invented historical sources or fabricated quotations presented as real.
hardFailConditions.notes: Severe academic integrity issue; apply major penalty and flag.

hardFailConditions.description: Hate speech, Holocaust denial, harassment, or demeaning content.

majorPenaltyConditions.description: More than 40% of body text is chronological retelling with minimal analysis.
No identifiable thesis or the 'thesis' is purely descriptive.
Any required section is missing or extremely underdeveloped (<50% of minWords).

minorPenaltyConditions.description: Frequent typos/grammar errors that distract but do not block understanding.
Sections present but headings unclear or inconsistent.

levels.descriptor: Clear, arguable thesis about WWII; strong roadmap; concise historical context.
Thesis is arguable; roadmap mostly clear; minor vagueness in scope.
Thesis present but broad or partly descriptive; weak roadmap.
Thesis hard to find or not arguable; intro mostly narrative.
No real thesis; introduction fails to set up argument.
Missing introduction/thesis section.
Accurate, objective, and within 250–400 words; sets up causes effectively.
Accurate; slightly outside target range or minor omissions.
Mostly accurate but too detailed or missing key pre-war elements.
Noticeable inaccuracies or excessive detail; unclear connection to thesis.
Largely inaccurate or far too long/short.
Missing historical background section.
Insightful multi-causal analysis (political, economic, ideological) with primary source evidence.
Good analysis; one cause dimension less developed or evidence thin.
Basic causes identified; limited interpretation or missing a required dimension.
Mostly narrative retelling; minimal causal analysis.
Superficial or inaccurate causal claims.
Missing cause analysis section.
Strong analysis of 2+ turning points; clear strategic reasoning; connected to thesis.
Clear turning points identified; one analysis underdeveloped.
Turning points listed but analysis is general or lacks dates/context.
Vague references to battles/events without strategic analysis.
Confused or inaccurate claims about key events.
Missing turning points section.
2+ primary and 3+ secondary sources smoothly integrated; each analyzed; citations consistent.
Meets source minimums; analysis present; minor integration/citation issues.
Sources present but analysis is thin or sources overused without interpretation.
Too few sources or citations dropped in without analysis.
Sources missing or citations absent.
No source evidence section or no citations.
Engages with alternative interpretations; rebuttal is evidence-based and nuanced.
Good counterargument; response could be deeper.
Counterargument mentioned but not fully developed or rebutted.
Thin or absent counterargument; mostly one-sided.
Misrepresents opposing views or provides straw-man argument.
Missing counterargument section.
Specific, thoughtful reflection; strong connection to modern geopolitics or conflict.
Clear reflection; connection present but could be deeper.
General reflection; vague modern connection.
Mostly summary; minimal reflection.
Off-topic or inappropriate tone.
Missing reflection section.
Clear academic tone; strong transitions; minimal errors.
Mostly clear; minor organization/mechanics issues.
Understandable but repetitive; noticeable grammar/structure issues.
Hard to follow; frequent errors; weak structure.
Very difficult to read or inappropriate tone.
Unscorable.

accuracyAssessment.purpose: Assess how accurate and reliable the grading is (meta-evaluation).

dimensions.name: Rubric Adherence
dimensions.description: Did the grader apply the stated requirements and scoring levels without inventing new rules?

dimensions.name: Evidence Grounding
dimensions.description: Does each score include concrete evidence snippets from the student essay?

dimensions.name: Uncertainty & Edge Cases
dimensions.description: Does the grader flag close calls and avoid overconfident claims?

dimensions.name: Safety / Bias / Professionalism
dimensions.description: Is the feedback respectful, non-discriminatory, and appropriate?
